Summary of what was found: ETMA’s tube overview provides the baseline of current European tube formats: aluminum, plastic, and laminate tubes, and explains where each material system is typically used. It also lists key European tube test standards for airtightness, weld strength, print adhesion, flip-top orientation, and dimensional tolerances.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: baseline tube formats and tube performance standards. Tube format relevance: aluminum tubes, plastic tubes, ABL laminate tubes, PBL/CBL laminate tubes. Weight/material reduction relevance: establishes the current tube material families that lightweighting options must compare against. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: plastic tubes are identified as highly important for cosmetics, while laminate tubes are used where barrier and print quality matter. Premium versus mass-market fit: applies to both. Barrier, compatibility, decoration, recyclability, filling-line, or consumer-experience limitation notes: lists relevant EN tests including airtightness of closures, head-weld strength, side-seam strength, print adhesion, flip-top orientation, and dimensional tolerances. Use in screening: use as the taxonomy and performance-test anchor for tube routes. Limitations/caution: European tube-market framing, not a global share estimate.
Source name: Always the optimal material for a good idea — ETMA — https://www.etma-online.org/tubetypes.html

Summary of what was found: ETMA’s recyclability position paper argues plastic tubes are lightweight and have a favorable packaging-weight-to-fill-volume ratio versus bottles, while recyclability depends on consistent design-for-recycling and post-consumer sorting/recycling.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: plastic tube lightweight/recyclability framing. Tube format relevance: extruded and laminate plastic tubes. Weight/material reduction evidence: plastic tubes are described as resource-efficient and lightweight with better packaging-weight-to-filling-volume ratio than bottles. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: supports tubes as an inherently attractive lightweight format for beauty and personal care. Premium versus mass-market fit: both, with mass-market strongest by scale. Barrier/recyclability limitation notes: recyclability depends on design-for-recycling plus efficient collection, sorting, and recycling systems. Use in screening: use to justify tubes as weight-efficient but not automatically recycling-ready. Limitations/caution: position paper from a tube association, so use with design-for-recycling guidance.
Source name: Position Paper on the Recyclability of Plastic Tubes — ETMA — https://linhardt.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Etma_position_paper_recyclability_of_plastic_tubes_revision_2024_final.pdf

Summary of what was found: APR’s Design Guide describes common HDPE and PP tube construction, typical squeeze-tube wall thicknesses, and frequent use of aluminum foil or EVOH barrier layers, pigments, fillers, printing, and inserts.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: HDPE and PP tube construction and design-for-recycling baseline. Tube format relevance: extruded tubes and laminated sheet/film tubes. Weight/material reduction evidence: typical squeeze-tube wall thickness ranges from 160 to 600 microns, useful for benchmarking downgauging. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: directly relevant to cosmetic and personal-care tubes using HDPE/PP and barrier layers. Premium versus mass-market fit: both. Barrier/recyclability limitation notes: barrier layers may use aluminum foil or EVOH; pigments, fillers, labels, printing, and inserts contribute to material system complexity. Use in screening: benchmark tube wall thickness and recognize sleeves, barriers, inserts, shoulders, and closures as mass contributors. Limitations/caution: design-for-recycling source, not a quantified lightweighting case.
Source name: The APR Design Guide for Plastics Recyclability — Association of Plastic Recyclers — https://plasticsrecycling.org/documents/res-o-01/

Summary of what was found: APR’s tube design resource makes closure choice a major recyclability issue: caps can be a significant share of the package, PP is a contaminant in the HDPE stream, and tube size/sortability thresholds matter.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: tube closure and sortability design-for-recycling guidance. Tube format relevance: HDPE and PP squeeze tubes with caps/closures. Weight/material reduction evidence: caps/closures can be a significant part of the package, so lightweighting and material matching should include closure systems. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: highly relevant because beauty tubes often use PP flip-top or screw caps on PE/HDPE tubes. Premium versus mass-market fit: both; high-volume personal care especially. Recyclability limitation notes: PP is a contaminant in the HDPE stream, preferred designs keep closure value in the recycling stream, and tube size/sortation matters. Use in screening: do not evaluate sleeves alone; cap material and sorting performance can make or break a recyclable lightweight tube route. Limitations/caution: recyclability guidance rather than tube weight case study.
Source name: Plastic Squeeze Tubes Design Resource Document HDPE and PP Tubes — Association of Plastic Recyclers — https://www.plasticsmarkets.org/jsfcontent/APRTubesDesign_jsf_1.pdf

Summary of what was found: RecyClass reports that Albéa’s HDPE tube with HDPE flip-top cap was approved, marking a notable milestone because the low-melt-flow HDPE flip-top cap technology tested successfully.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: mono-material HDPE tube-plus-cap system. Tube format relevance: HDPE tubes with HDPE flip-top caps. Weight/material reduction evidence: not a direct weight-saving case, but supports mono-material redesign without losing closure functionality. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: strong for recyclable beauty and personal-care tubes. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and masstige; premium if appearance/feel passes. Recyclability limitation notes: RecyClass approval gives European direction for improving recyclability of rigid HDPE packaging. Use in screening: move mono-HDPE tube/closure combinations into detailed evaluation and avoid default PP caps where HDPE recycling compatibility is the objective. Limitations/caution: approval is for a tested technology and should not be generalized to all HDPE caps without validation.
Source name: Albéa’s HDPE tube with HDPE flip-top cap approved by RecyClass — RecyClass — https://recyclass.eu/news/albeas-hdpe-tube-with-hdpe-flip-top-cap-approved-by-recyclass/

Summary of what was found: Neopac’s Lightweight Tube quantifies a downgauging route: tube body wall thickness reduced from 0.5 mm to 0.35 mm, cutting virgin material by up to 30% while maintaining a similar haptic experience.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: downgauged PE/coex tube sleeves. Tube format relevance: Mono and Coex tubes for skincare, hair care, make-up, toothpaste, and dentifrice. Weight/material reduction evidence: wall thickness reduced from 0.5 mm to 0.35 mm; up to 30% virgin material reduction; for one million D50/210 mm sleeve-only tubes, up to 4.6 tons HDPE eliminated and about 1.5 tons CO2 reduced. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: direct cosmetic and personal-care tube lightweighting evidence. Premium versus mass-market fit: core mass-market route; premium/luxe may require haptic validation because feel is described as similar, not identical. Barrier/filling limitation notes: optional EVOH and HDPE/rHDPE shoulders with HDPE or PP low-profile closures. Use in screening: place downgauged PE/coex tubes into later evaluation. Limitations/caution: not a full formula/filling-line matrix.

Summary of what was found: Neopac’s Mono PE Tube PDF explains the recyclability problem with PE tubes using PP caps and positions HDPE caps as functionally equivalent and visually similar, enabling mono-material PE tubes.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: mono-PE tube with HDPE cap/closure. Tube format relevance: PE tubes, flip-top caps, screw caps, PE shoulders. Weight/material reduction evidence: not a numeric weight reduction source; relevant because closure material choice affects both material system and recyclability. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: highly relevant to recyclable cosmetic and personal-care tubes. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market, masstige, and premium where HDPE cap appearance meets expectations. Recyclability/consumer limitation notes: PP caps seal well and look good but create mixed-material tubes; HDPE caps are positioned as high quality and well sealing. Use in screening: prioritize PE sleeves with HDPE caps for recyclable mono-material tube systems when seal testing confirms performance. Limitations/caution: supplier PDF; brand-specific closure feel and torque validation still needed.
Source name: Mono PE Tube — Hoffmann Neopac — https://www.neopac.com/en/pdf?id=9813

Summary of what was found: Neopac’s recycled tube platform shows PCR-rich tube bodies can coexist with cosmetic relevance, thin walls, recyclable rHDPE closures, optional EVOH barrier, and decoration options.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: PCR PE/rPE tubes with rHDPE shoulder/closure options. Tube format relevance: recycled-content tubes with 0.35/0.40/0.50 mm wall thickness, optional EVOH, decoration options. Weight/material reduction evidence: supports PCR plus thin-wall options rather than treating recycled content separately from lightweighting. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: references cosmetic/skincare suitability and quality for cosmetic products. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass and masstige skincare/personal care; premium if appearance consistency and decoration pass. Barrier/decoration limitation notes: appearance consistency, hot-foil/print quality, and barrier needs should be checked case by case. Use in screening: include PCR PE tubes as mainstream candidates with performance validation. Limitations/caution: not a full independent performance dataset.

Summary of what was found: EPL’s Platina shows recyclable laminated tubes can retain barrier performance: the laminate uses less than 5% barrier resin, meets stringent APR HDPE guidance, and is positioned as avoiding the usual trade-off between recyclability and shelf life.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: recyclable HDPE laminate tube with low barrier-resin content. Tube format relevance: laminate tubes for personal care, toiletries, and cosmetics. Weight/material reduction evidence: source-reduction and recyclable laminate route rather than sleeve downgauging; uses less than 5% barrier resin. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: strong for applications needing barrier plus recyclability. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and masstige; premium if aesthetics and haptics pass. Barrier/recyclability limitation notes: designed to preserve barrier properties, product stability, and shelf life while remaining APR-recognized in HDPE streams. Use in screening: shortlist recyclable HDPE laminate tubes where simple mono-layer PE is insufficient. Limitations/caution: supplier platform, brand-specific product stability still required.

Summary of what was found: EPL’s r-Platina extends the laminated-tube route by combining recyclability with higher PCR content and thinner laminates: 250μ and 300μ options with up to 50% PCR and 60% PCR respectively, plus RecyClass A certification and premium-performance positioning.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: recyclable PCR laminate tubes. Tube format relevance: 250 micron and 300 micron laminate tube constructions. Weight/material reduction evidence: thinner laminate options and high PCR content; up to 50% PCR in 250μ and up to 60% PCR in 300μ. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: applicable to cosmetics, oral care, baby care, and household products. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass and premium where PCR laminate aesthetics and performance satisfy brand needs. Barrier/decoration limitation notes: supplier claims premium performance and aesthetics; validate decoration, squeeze feel, and formula compatibility. Use in screening: screen as recyclable laminate plus PCR plus downgauging route. Limitations/caution: supplier claim and product-specific validation needed.

Summary of what was found: Berry’s Slimline closure launch confirms closure lightweighting is a distinct active route: the range is described as one of the lightest on the market, available in PE/PP or food-safe rPE/rPP, with mono-material pairing possible.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: lightweight low-profile tube closures. Tube format relevance: closures for skincare, body wash, hair care, and pharma tubes. Weight/material reduction evidence: directional lightweight design and GHG savings versus traditional caps, but no gram delta. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: strong because cap reductions scale in high-volume personal care. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and masstige; premium if closure feel and visual quality pass. Recyclability limitation notes: virgin or recycled PE/PP options allow material matching with tube materials for mono-material packs. Use in screening: treat closure lightweighting as a stand-alone workstream. Limitations/caution: no quantified cap weight reduction.
Source name: Berry Launches New Lightweight Tube Closure Solution — Berry Global — https://www.berryglobal.com/en/news/articles/berry-launches-new-lightweight-tube-closure-solution

Summary of what was found: Aptar positions Purity Lite as the first low-profile tube top in North America and says it becomes a lightweight, recyclable mono-material solution when paired with HDPE tubes; Aptar emphasizes reducing weight and minimizing extra materials.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: low-profile tube top/closure system. Tube format relevance: HDPE tubes and closures/tops. Weight/material reduction evidence: positioned as lightweight and material-minimizing but not quantified. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: closure determines consumer experience and recycling compatibility in personal-care tubes. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market, masstige, and selected premium if top design is acceptable. Recyclability limitation notes: mono-material when paired with HDPE tubes; also reduces extra materials such as liners and shrink wrappers. Use in screening: include low-profile mono-material closure systems in shortlist and follow with mass, torque, seal, and consumer-use validation. Limitations/caution: strategic source rather than highly quantitative.
Source name: Enabling Brands to Achieve Sustainability Targets and Unlock Growth Opportunities — Aptar — https://aptar.com/en-us/news-events/enabling-brands-to-achieve-sustainability-targets-and-unlock-growth-opportunities

Summary of what was found: Albéa’s EcoFusion Top is a quantified tube-system lightweighting example: by merging head and cap into one part, it claims over 80% weight reduction versus standard head+cap and 55% reduction for the full tube, while remaining HDPE mono-material and ISTA-6 e-commerce ready.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: integrated tube head/cap lightweight system. Tube format relevance: D50/200 ml style cosmetic/personal-care tubes with thin-wall sleeve plus integrated top. Weight/material reduction evidence: over 80% weight reduction versus standard head+cap and 55% full-tube reduction versus standard tube in cited example. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: shows tube lightweighting can come from closure/shoulder architecture, not sleeve alone. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong for mass-market and selected premium where visible closure redesign is acceptable. Barrier/filling/e-commerce limitation notes: HDPE mono-material and ISTA-6 e-commerce readiness are noted; classic cap aesthetics may be a barrier. Use in screening: evaluate tubes by full system: sleeve plus shoulder/head/cap. Limitations/caution: proprietary design and visible pack architecture change.
Source name: Albéa’s EcoFusion Top range - Less plastic for a reduced carbon footprint — Cosmetics Business — https://cosmeticsbusiness.com/alb-a-s-ecofusion-top-range-less-plastic-for-a-reduced-carbon-footprint-209313

Summary of what was found: L’Oréal’s announcement with Albéa provides major-beauty-company proof for paper-based tube development, stating that the first cosmetic tube integrating certified paper-based material was designed to replace the majority of plastic currently used in such tubes.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: paper-based cosmetic tube development. Tube format relevance: face and body care tubes. Weight/material reduction evidence: designed to replace the majority of plastic currently used in such tubes, but no precise gram reduction. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: strategic beauty-industry proof that paper/plastic hybrid tubes are being developed. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/luxe, natural-positioned beauty, and selected personal care where paper tactility adds value. Barrier/filling limitation notes: announcement lacks technical details on barrier, filling-line behavior, or scale economics. Use in screening: keep paper/plastic hybrid tubes on longlist but not default mainstream shortlist. Limitations/caution: strategic announcement, not a detailed performance case.
Source name: L’Oréal and Albéa launch the first cosmetic tube integrating certified paper-based material — L’Oréal Groupe — https://www.loreal.com/en/news/commitments/loreal-and-albea-launch-the-first-paper-based-cosmetic-tube/

Summary of what was found: Neopac’s PaperX gives a quantified paper-hybrid example for cosmetics: 80% FSC paper, 46% less plastic than standard COEX tubes, 24% lower CO2, 300-micron wall, EVOH/coating barrier, and compatibility with standard sealing equipment.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: paper-hybrid cosmetic tube with barrier layers. Tube format relevance: 300-micron fiber-based laminate tubes for cosmetics/personal care. Weight/material reduction evidence: 46% less plastic than standard COEX tubes and 24% lower CO2. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: meaningful for premium/natural beauty and selected personal-care products where tactile differentiation matters. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/natural-positioned conditional route; mass-market possible only if cost and line compatibility work. Barrier/filling limitation notes: uses EVOH plus coating barrier, PP shoulder/closure, and standard sealing equipment; paper-content claims should not be confused with full paper-stream simplicity. Use in screening: evaluate carefully as a differentiated lightweight route. Limitations/caution: still relies on plastic layers and plastic shoulder/cap in this version.

Summary of what was found: Neopac’s PaperX FiberTop pushes paper further by moving fiber into body, shoulder, and cap, with about 85% fiber overall, up to 87% less plastic, EVOH in body/shoulder, and standard filling/end-sealing compatibility, but it is still presented as an emerging pilot path.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: fiber-forward tube with paper body, shoulder, and closure. Tube format relevance: paper-based tubes for cosmetics/personal care. Weight/material reduction evidence: up to 87% less plastic and up to 65% lower emissions versus standard Polyfoil or COEX tubes; technical highlights include around 80% paper body, 85% fiber shoulder, and 95% fiber cap. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: high-interest innovation track for brands seeking paper tactility and major plastic reduction. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium innovation/future-route first, not default mass-market rollout. Barrier/filling limitation notes: includes thin EVOH barriers and is described as compatible with standard filling and end-sealing, but is in pilot/industrialization stage. Use in screening: place in future-route/premium innovation bucket. Limitations/caution: not yet safest near-term high-volume route.

Summary of what was found: Albéa’s Timeless PCR PBL is an example of a lighter laminate tube with recycled content: around 30% recycled material in total, 60% recycled content in the sleeve, 17% lower carbon footprint versus other recycled tubes, and suitability for shower, shampoo, and sun-care formulas due to light/moisture barrier.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: PCR plastic-barrier laminate tubes. Tube format relevance: laminate tubes for shower, shampoo, and sun-care products. Weight/material reduction evidence: uses less material and has 17% lower carbon footprint versus other recycled tubes; around 30% recycled material overall and 60% recycled sleeve content. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: strong for personal care and skincare subcategories that need better product protection than a basic mono-layer tube. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and masstige transitional route. Barrier limitation notes: offers high light barrier and superior moisture barrier; pure mono-material or paper-heavy options may not satisfy same protection needs. Use in screening: treat as practical transitional format where high barrier and recycled content matter. Limitations/caution: not necessarily the final optimum for recyclability.
Source name: Timeless: PCR Laminate Tubes for Cosmetics by Albéa — Global Cosmetic Industry — https://www.gcimagazine.com/packaging/news/21863240/albea-timeless-pcr-post-consumer-recycled-laminate-tubes-for-cosmetics-by-albea

Summary of what was found: Neopac’s aluminum-barrier discussion is a necessary caution source: ABLs are difficult to recycle in current systems, but mono-material alternatives will not eliminate ABL entirely because aluminum barriers are still needed for the most stringent barrier applications.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: aluminum-barrier laminate tubes and substitution limits. Tube format relevance: ABL tubes and mono-material barrier alternatives. Weight/material reduction evidence: not a weight-saving source; informs caution around composite-heavy structures. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: relevant to products needing volatile retention, stringent barrier, or regulated ointment/cream-like performance. Premium versus mass-market fit: ABL should be flagged as heavier/harder-to-recycle but not automatically rejected when barrier needs are critical. Recyclability/barrier limitation notes: ABL generally cannot be industrially separated/recycled in current facilities, but may remain necessary for the most stringent barrier applications. Use in screening: treat ABL as substitution candidate where feasible, but retain it in special barrier-required cases. Limitations/caution: supplier perspective and barrier need should be validated product by product.
Source name: Closing the loop with aluminum barrier tubes — Hoffmann Neopac — https://www.neopac.com/en/news-events/closing-the-loop-with-aluminum-barrier-tubes
